sourceTitle: Two note taking systems to make your engineering team more transparent & productive

questions.question: According to the guide, which of the following is NOT a direct benefit of good engineering notes?

options.text: They reduce the time spent hunting for information.
options.explanation: Good notes are easy to find, so this is a real benefit.

options.text: They help engineers ship faster and with higher quality.
options.explanation: The guide explicitly links good notes to speed and quality.

options.text: They automatically generate code for the project.
options.explanation: Notes do not generate code; the guide never claims this.

options.text: They provide a record of past decisions to avoid duplicate effort.
options.explanation: Maintaining a decision history is a highlighted advantage.

questions.question: When setting up a Meeting Notes database, which property type would you use to tag the participants of a meeting?

options.explanation: Select is for single‑choice categories, not for tagging people.
The Person property lets you tag individual participants.
Date records timestamps, not people.
Number is for numeric values, not participant names.

questions.question: Which view type would be most useful if you want to see all meeting notes grouped by their Type (e.g., Standup, Sprint Planning, Post‑Mortem)?

options.explanation: Calendar groups by date, not by a custom property.
Board view can group cards by any select property, such as Type.
List view can filter but doesn't visually group by Type.
Gallery is for visual cards; grouping by Type still requires a board.

questions.question: In the Daily Standup template suggested by the guide, which of the following headings is NOT included?

options.explanation: This is a required heading in the standup template.
Blockers are explicitly listed in the template.
Sprint Goal belongs to the Sprint Planning template, not Standup.
Action items are part of the standup template.

questions.question: Which property combination best supports tracking the lifecycle of a Docs entry (e.g., a Technical Spec) as described in the guide?

options.explanation: These capture category and creation date but not status or review progress.
Status shows progress but alone doesn't identify the doc type or timestamps.

options.text: Type (Select), Status (Select), Created time, Last edited time
options.explanation: All four together give category, current stage, and temporal context.

options.text: Created by and Last updated by
options.explanation: These give ownership but not the doc’s stage or type.

questions.question: If you wanted to see only the Docs that you personally authored, which filter configuration would you apply?

options.explanation: Filtering by the Creator property set to your own user shows only your docs.
This filters by document category, not by author.
Sorting changes order but does not restrict to your own docs.
Grouping organizes by status; it does not filter by author.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of adding a Relation property called "Project" to the Meeting Notes database?

options.text: To automatically generate meeting agendas based on project milestones.
options.explanation: Relations link records; they don’t auto‑generate content.

options.text: To link each meeting note to the relevant project so both sides can view each other’s information.
options.explanation: The guide states this creates a two‑way sync between notes and projects.

options.text: To replace the need for a separate Docs database.
options.explanation: Relation complements Docs; it doesn’t replace a Docs database.

options.text: To enable real‑time chat between participants within the meeting note.
options.explanation: Relations do not provide chat functionality.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes why the guide recommends hiding irrelevant properties in a List view?

options.text: It improves page load speed by reducing database size.
options.explanation: Hiding properties doesn’t affect the underlying database size.

options.text: It declutters the view so users can focus on the most important information at a glance.
options.explanation: The guide emphasizes clarity and focus by showing only key properties.

options.text: It prevents other team members from editing those hidden properties.
options.explanation: Hidden properties remain editable; they’re just not displayed.

options.text: It automatically archives older records associated with hidden properties.
options.explanation: Archiving is a separate action; hiding does not archive records.

questions.question: When creating a Post‑Mortem template, the guide suggests adding a table of contents using which Notion command?

options.explanation: The guide explicitly mentions inserting a table of contents with the /toc command.
Heading creates headings, not a TOC.
Link inserts hyperlinks, not a table of contents.
Embed adds external content; it does not generate a TOC.

questions.question: The guide lists several steps to get the whole team into the habit of using the new system. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a habit‑building tactic?

options.text: Adding a description with quick instructions at the top of the database.
options.explanation: This is listed as a way to surface usage instructions.

options.text: Creating pre‑made views tailored to different sub‑teams before sharing.
options.explanation: Tailored views are recommended to demonstrate immediate value.

options.text: Running a weekly quiz on the system’s features.
options.explanation: The guide never mentions quizzes as a habit‑building method.

options.text: Live‑demoing template usage during a meeting.
options.explanation: Demonstrating templates in real time is suggested.
